title: Lesson 4: Sharing Your Story and Jesus' Story
description: The STORY rhythm of BLESS comes to life. Learn to craft your personal testimony and clearly share the gospel using tools you can use anywhere.

blocks.text: Over the last three lessons you have built a foundation for living on mission. Lesson 1 gave you the vision and your Personal Ministry Map. Lesson 2 gave you BLESS - five rhythms for everyday mission. Lesson 3 showed you how your table and your home are frontline tools for the gospel. Now this final lesson gives you the words. This is the STORY rhythm of BLESS fully unpacked: how to share what Jesus has done in your life, and how to explain the gospel clearly enough that anyone can understand it.
Why Your Story Matters

Here is a simple definition of witnessing: Witnessing is taking a good look at the Lord Jesus and then telling others what you have seen. That is it. You do not need a theology degree or a list of memorized arguments. You need your experience of Jesus, clearly articulated, ready to share.

People are drawn to stories. They may push back on doctrine or tune out a theological argument, but they almost never argue with someone's genuine experience. When you say, "Here is what my life was like, here is what happened, here is what is different now" - that is personal, honest, and hard to dismiss. Your testimony is one of the most powerful evangelism tools you have. And unlike a gospel diagram that you need to learn, your testimony is something you already own. You just need to organize it.

But in your hearts honor Christ the Lord as holy, always being prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you; yet do it with gentleness and respect, having a good conscience, so that, when you are slandered, those who revile your good behavior in Christ may be put to shame.

Peter says always be prepared - not always be presenting. Preparation is the internal work. You develop your story so that when the moment comes - when a friend asks why you seem different, when a coworker asks what keeps you grounded, when a neighbor opens up about a hard season - you are ready. Not polished. Not perfect. Just ready.
4.1 - How comfortable do you feel sharing your faith with others right now? Rate yourself 1-10. What makes you comfortable or uncomfortable about it?
The Three Barriers
There are three common reasons people hesitate to share their faith. See if you recognize yourself in any of them:

items: Fear of rejection. "What if they think I am weird?" Remember: when someone rejects the gospel, they are rejecting Jesus, not you. Your job is to offer - the response is between them and God.
Not knowing what to say. "I am not a preacher. I do not know the Bible well enough." That is why you prepare. This lesson gives you a framework you can practice until it feels natural.
No models. "I have never seen anyone do it in a way that was not awkward." Spend time with people who share their faith naturally. Watch how they do it - often it is far simpler than you think.

blocks.text: 4.2 - Which of the three barriers resonates most with you? What is one practical step you could take to begin overcoming it?
Tool 1: Write Your Testimony
Your personal testimony can be as simple as three short paragraphs and should be shareable in 3-5 minutes. Think of it as three movements of one story:

items: Paragraph 1: Your life before Christ. Tell a little of your background. Did you grow up in a church family? Were you far from God? What did life look like - what were your struggles, your attitude, what was missing?

Paragraph 2: Your decision to follow Jesus. How did you come to understand what Jesus had done for you? Who told you? When and where did it happen? What clicked? Include some points of the gospel: Jesus died for you, He rose again, and you put your faith in Him.

Paragraph 3: Your life now. What difference has Jesus made in your life? If the difference has been gradual, say that. If dramatic, tell the story. What are you looking forward to in your relationship with God? What does following Jesus look like for you right now?

blocks.text: A helpful tip: feel free to include a Bible verse that was meaningful in your decision or Christian life. Not a whole passage - just a verse that anchored you. Something you can share naturally.
[PLACEHOLDER: Testimony Framework infographic - three panels showing Before / Decision / Now with prompts in each]

4.3 - Write your testimony here using the three-paragraph framework above. Write it in plain, ordinary language - the way you would tell a friend over coffee. This is your first draft - you can refine it later.
4.4 - What did you learn about yourself as you wrote down your testimony? What opportunities could you use your story to share God's story and impact another person's story?
Tool 2: Share the Gospel Clearly

Your testimony opens the door. But there may come a moment when someone needs to hear not just your story, but His story - the gospel itself, clear and simple. For that, you need a framework. Something you can explain in a few minutes and something the person you share it with could turn around and share with someone else.
How then will they call on him in whom they have not believed? And how are they to believe in him of whom they have never heard? And how are they to hear without someone preaching?
The gospel has a shape. It moves through four essential movements, and every good gospel presentation covers all four. Leaving one out creates confusion:

items: God's Design. God made us for relationship with Him. We were created in His image, for His glory, to know Him and be known by Him.

Our Problem. Sin broke the relationship. Every person has chosen their own way over God's way, and the result is separation - not just moral failure, but relational distance from the God we were made for.

God's Solution. God did not leave us in the gap. He sent Jesus - fully God, fully human - to live the life we could not and die the death we deserved. The resurrection proves that death did not have the final word.
Our Response. The gospel is not just information to agree with. It requires a response: turn from sin and trust in Jesus. This is the hinge between knowing the story and being part of it.

blocks.text: Here are two tools you can use to practice sharing these four movements visually. Both are designed to be drawn on a napkin, a whiteboard, or shared from your phone:
The Gospel Diagram - A visual tool that walks through the four movements of the gospel. Watch the training video and practice drawing it: ministrybag.com/thegospeldiagram
[PLACEHOLDER: Gospel Diagram image - a simple visual showing God's Design / Brokenness / Jesus / Response]
3 Circles - Another powerful way to share the gospel visually, developed by the North American Mission Board. Learn and practice it here: namb.net/evangelism/3circles

You do not need to master both. Pick one. Learn it well enough that you could draw it from memory and walk someone through it naturally. Then practice - with a Christian friend first, then with someone on your Personal Ministry Map.
4.5 - In your own words, walk through each of the four gospel movements as if you were explaining them to a friend over coffee. Don't copy the text above - say it the way YOU would say it.
4.6 - What was your experience watching or practicing the Gospel Diagram or 3 Circles? Which one felt more natural to you and why?
Faithful, Not Successful

Here is something that will take the pressure off: "successful evangelism" is not dependent on the response of the person, but on your faithfulness and boldness to offer the Good News. Everyone is on their own path, taking steps each day toward or away from a decision to follow Christ. When you share your faith, you become a step in their story. You may be the first step or the tenth. You may be the one who plants and someone else waters. Only God makes things grow (1 Corinthians 3:6-7). God gets the glory, and you get the joy of offering His grace and truth to others.
And they have conquered him by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony, for they loved not their lives even unto death.
The blood of the Lamb and the word of your testimony. Those are the two weapons. Jesus already supplied the first one. The second one is yours to use.
BLESS Review and Personal Ministry Check-In

You have now learned all five BLESS rhythms in detail. You have a Personal Ministry Map, a hospitality strategy, a written testimony, and a gospel tool. Look back at where you started in Lesson 1. Look at the names you wrote down. The mission is not abstract anymore - it has names and faces.
4.7 - Look back at your prayer list from Lesson 2 and your Personal Ministry Map from Lesson 1. Who are you currently BLESSing? What is working? What has been harder than you expected?

4.8 - Pray and ask God to highlight one person from your map. Write their name. Now write what the situation and conversation might look like if you shared your testimony and the gospel with them. Where would you be? What would you say? What questions might they have?
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
